data.title: Politics And The Markets 08/24/25
data.description:
9This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: WM Technology Could Double
data.description:
I am cautious on cannabis stocks, but one that I really like is WM Technology (NASDAQ:MAPS). It has rallied a bit since I last wrote about it here at Seeking Alpha in June, suggesting it was a good stock for cannabis investors, but cannabis stocks have soared since then. In this follow-up, I reiterate my Strong Buy, discuss Q2 and the outlook, review the chart, and explain why I have a 20% position in my model portfolio at 420 Investor.

MAPS Had Good Financial Results in Q2

The company reported its Q2 on 8/7. Revenue of $44.8 million was down 2% from the prior year and in line with the guidance it had provided when it shared Q1 financial results. It had guided for adjusted EBITDA of $8 million, and the $11.7 million reported for Q2 exceeded its forecast and grew 16% from a year earlier. Operating cash flow was $11 million during Q2, compared to $5.7 million in Q1 and $12.7 million a year earlier. Capital spending of $6.5 million in H1 was less than the $16.7 million in operating cash flow.

In addition to good operational results, the company reported an even stronger balance sheet that has no debt. The cash increased to $59 million from $53.3 million at the end of Q1 and $52 million at year-end. A year earlier, it was $41.3 million. I use tangible book value to assess down-side risk, and the tangible book value of MAPS increased again to $57.8 million.

The Outlook Did Change in the Short-Term

In the press release, the company guided for revenue in Q3 of $41-43 million with adjusted EBITDA of $5-7 million. Both of these are down sequentially, and the revenue would decline by 8–12% from a year earlier. In 2024-Q3, reported adjusted EBITDA was $11.3 million, so the range provided for this quarter this year is quite lower but still positive.

MAPS is not widely followed by analysts. I am aware of two that currently provide research on it: Pablo Zuanic of Zuanic & Associates (formerly covering the cannabis industry at Cantor Fitzgerald) and Casey Ryan of Westpark. I have read Zuanic's research piece that he wrote after the quarter, providing updates, and I was able to access information about Ryan's outlook. Ryan apparently has a target of $2.61. Zuanic, who discloses that MAPS is a paying customer of his firm, changed his forecasts for 2025, 2026, and 2027:

While he cut his revenue estimates for 2025 and 2026 slightly, he boosted his 2027 outlook substantially. For adjusted EBITDA, he raised his 2025 projection, kept his 2026 forecast similar to his prior estimate, and boosted his 2027 outlook.

I like Zuanic's forecasts here. Before the Q2 report, I was assuming $30 million adjusted EBITDA for 2025 and then $35 million for 2026. My 2026 outlook for MAPS was based on a 17% margin for what I thought revenue could be that year (I was using the Koyfin consensus revenue of $211 million). Koyfin now has a consensus 2026 revenue of $188 million, and the 17% margin would be $32.0 million, slightly above Zuanic's forecast, which works out to a 16.9% margin on his own outlook. His boost in 2027 works out to a 16.9% margin as well.

Note that the company, which is reporting positive EPS now, has a consensus to report EPS of $0.16 in 2025, $0.17 in 2026, and $0.20 in 2027, according to Koyfin as well as Seeking Alpha.

MAPS Could Benefit From Cannabis Rescheduling

Cannabis rescheduling is not a done deal, but it could eliminate 280E taxation for American cannabis growers and sellers. 280E taxation has been weighing on cannabis companies, forcing them to pay income tax effectively that is much higher than normal tax rates.

Green Thumb Industries (OTCQX:GTBIF), which I like now relative to its peers, disclosed an effective tax rate of 96.7% in Q2 and 85.3% in H1. Many of its peers have income tax provisions that exceed their net income.

MAPS does not pay 280E taxes, but its customers are legally required to do so. They also struggle with debt, which has high interest rates and is tough to roll over.

If cannabis gets rescheduled from Schedule 1 to Schedule 3, 280E taxation would end. Of course, Congress could impose a new tax. So, it is not yet known that 280E taxation will be ending, but it would be very positive for spending by cannabis companies if it were to go away.

MAPS Is an Extremely Cheap Stock

MAPS has 107.2 million shares outstanding and also 49.3 million Class V shares. This totals 156.5 million. I do add 13.8 million Class P shares, 9.3 million RSUs, and 4.34 million PSUs to give a total effective share count of 184 million. There are 19.5 million $11.50 warrants outstanding too. At $1.12, I calculate a market cap of $206.1 million. Subtracting the cash, the enterprise value is $147.2 million.

Using the 2025 projected adjusted EBITDA, the enterprise value is just 4.1X Zuanic's 2025 projected adjusted EBITDA, which is very low for a NASDAQ-listed company that is operating federally legally. The 2025 PE is just 7X.

My target ahead of the Q2 report for year-end was $2.04, which was based on an enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA in 2026 of 8X. For year-end, adjusting for the lower projected adjusted EBITDA than I was assuming, the target would now be $1.65. While this is lower than my year-end target had been, it is 47% above the current price.

Today, I am sharing my look for the year ahead. I am using a multiple now of 10X and basing it on 50% of the 2026 projected adjusted EBITDA and 50% of the 2027 projected adjusted EBITDA. Using Zuanic's estimates, this works out to be projected adjusted EBITDA of $33.7 million. The target, assuming no change in shares outstanding or in cash (which could rise), is $2.15, 92% higher. Again, if 280E taxation goes away, revenue and adjusted EBITDA and EPS could increase for MAPS.

There are very few cannabis companies reporting profits these days, but MAPS is. My target a year out works out to be a 2026 PE of just 12.6X.

The MAPS Chart Says Buy

MAPS is down 18.8% in 2025, which is worse than the market. The New Cannabis Ventures Global Cannabis Stock Index, which does include MAPS, has increased 2.5% so far this year. Here is the one-year chart for MAPS:

Let me start with the negative: MAPS did gap up on 8/11, when the market started to process again potential rescheduling of cannabis. This gap is down to $0.95. Of course there is another gap down to $0.77 from November 2024, when the company reported its Q3.

There is a lot of good that I see, but first I need to remind readers about the big run-up that started in December 2024, when the company announced that insiders had $1.70 bid. I was not recommending the stock then and never believed that the transaction would take place. It was dropped officially in June after I wrote my Strong Buy piece. I said in that article that it was a risk that the deal would not go through.

The low last November of $0.70 was not the all-time low, which was $0.60 in early 2023. I do like the higher lows that have been set over the past year. I see support currently at $0.90-.95 and note that the 50-day moving average, just below $1.00, could provide support too.

Cannabis stocks have soared since 8/11, and there was some run-up ahead of then. Looking at the action since 6/30, the Global Cannabis Stock Index has increased 40.4%. The Q3 gains have been very high for MSOs, with the AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS) more than doubling, a much-bigger increase than MAPS enjoyed:

MSOS has seen its share count increase by 26% so far in Q3. It had experienced some redemptions earlier in the year. I think that MSOS, which I have rated Strong Sell, is very risky. If 280E does not go away, it will likely face redemptions again.

There Are Risks to MAPS

While MAPS has closed above $1 since 8/11. It's testing that level and could face delisting if it can't hold it. It did not receive a warning in July or August, but NASDAQ rules state that if a stock closes below $1.00 for 30 consecutive business days, it can be delisted. The process takes 180 days from when the company is warned, and it can be extended with an appeal. MAPS did receive a warning in October 2024 but was able to close above $1.00 within 180 days.

Another potential challenge to MAPS is that if 280E taxation remains, its customer base is very weak and may continue to resist spending for its technology. MAPS has not made any attempt to move beyond the cannabis industry.

I don't think that either of these risks should keep investors away from the stock, but both could weigh on the stock, making it get even cheaper.

Conclusion

MAPS serves a very challenged industry, and it has held up well this quarter, rising 25% in price. The stocks of cannabis companies have soared recently on the news of potential cannabis rescheduling, but MAPS has lagged. The federally legal NASDAQ-listed stock seems cheap, and the company has no debt burdens. My target is much higher than the current price and could be far exceeded if 280E taxation goes away.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: D-Wave Quantum: Too Much Cash To Ignore
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Markets are heading into the last lap of the Q2 earnings season, having put up a reasonable performance so far through the quarter.

But the current chatter about the AI bubble has put a lid on the enthusiasm markets had demonstrated at the start of this month. Getting caught in the crosshairs of the concerns surrounding the AI bubble are the four biggest quantum computing stocks, which typically also get attached to the broader trade of emerging technologies like AI.

In this past week, during which concerns about the AI bubble grew, the four big quantum stocks fell and are poised to collectively shed at least $2B in market capitalization.

Of the four quantum stocks, D-Wave Quantum Inc. (NYSE:QBTS) got hit the hardest, falling over 11% week-to-date. But that could also be because D-Wave’s stock is scoring the biggest YTD gains for the year, up 79%, outpacing every other quantum stock by a large margin.

There is one other area that D-Wave outperforms the others—cash.

This could be a huge tailwind for D-Wave, as I discuss below, but there are some risks due to which I advise a Hold rating for the company.

Quick Primer On D-Wave Quantum’s Business Model

In the current state of computing, as we know it, information is created, processed, and consumed to provide value. The basic units of information that are created are called bits, and over time, computing systems have rapidly evolved, becoming more powerful and efficient at solving computational problems based on bits.

In quantum computing, this can all be sped up much faster due to the creation of qubits, the quantum equivalent of bits, but it needs quantum computing systems to create and process qubits. Today, the four most popular pure-play quantum computing companies publicly listed on markets have their own approaches to quantum computing.

D-Wave differentiates itself from the rest of the pure-play quantum space in three ways.

First is D-Wave’s unique approach to solving the quantum computational problem by leaning on a process called quantum annealing that helps the company and its clients solve optimization problems using quantum compute methods. The company follows a cloud software-style business model where it sells access to its quantum systems via the QCaaS (quantum compute as a service).

Second, D-Wave prides itself on being the only publicly listed pure-play quantum company that generates revenue from commercial sales of its quantum systems, unlike its peers that still heavily rely on government grants and endowments. It counts companies such as Mastercard (MA) as its clients.

And third is D-Wave’s massive cash hoard, at least 50% bigger than any of its peers.

D-Wave’s Cash Hoard Is Too Big Too Ignore

After factoring in the Q2 ER updates from all the pure-play quantum compute companies, I observe that D-Wave has worked up the biggest cash war chest of all—$819M in cash and ST investments. This is a record cash hoard for D-Wave and is at least 50% higher than the cash held by any of its peers. IonQ, Inc. (IONQ), a quantum peer, comes the closest to D-Wave with its own cash stockpile of $547M in cash.

The record cash D-Wave has secured for itself this year cushions the company from the $112M in annualized expenses, gross and operating costs, it burns through the year.

Per my estimates of annualized costs and current cash volumes on its balance sheet, D-Wave’s cash creates a strong runway of ~97 months of cash it can burn, which is a significant cushion for the company, in my opinion.

On the Q2 call with analysts, D-Wave’s management advised that part of the reason to build up its cash pile was to give the company some runway in ramping up expenses for four main areas—R&D, wafer fabrication & chip packaging, sales & marketing, and infrastructure.

But there is a strong reason why the company is planning to ramp up expenses. I noted earlier that D-Wave is possibly the only company to see commercial viability in its QCaaS business model, and their Q2 ER showed that bookings jumped 92% y/y to $1.3M, accelerating from both the previous Q1 CY25 quarter and the Q2 CY24 year. The combination of y/y acceleration and sequential acceleration is the holy grail of growth that markets look for, and D-Wave demonstrated that in the Q2 quarter.

The company did reveal that most of the bookings involve companies signing small contracts to get access to D-Wave’s QCaaS quantum system to evaluate the feasibility of applying D-Wave’s quantum computing systems to their business problems. If the dollar value of contracts that D-Wave’s management implies were just “small amounts,” then D-Wave must have signed on a large volume of new labels due to the 92% jump in bookings that they delivered in Q2.

Also, management did categorically state that they plan to deploy a part of their $819M in cash towards pursuing their M&A strategy, which has “now become a strategic priority for the company.”

D-Wave definitely looks well positioned to grab this opportunity that lies ahead of them to win more commercial deals and push the barriers of what is commercially possible with their QCaaS business model, especially with the strong cash hoard that they have.

D-Wave’s Setup Looks Interesting From A Valuation Standpoint

In response to the strong performance that D-Wave has put up through this year, analysts have raised their estimates on D-Wave, with consensus estimates calling for D-Wave’s CY25 revenues to be ~162% y/y, at the midpoint of those consensus estimates.

For that kind of expected growth, D-Wave trades at a forward valuation (EV/revenues) of 182x CY25 revenues.

If I compare D-Wave’s current sales multiple & growth pace to NVIDIA’s (NVDA) pricing scenario in CY23, D-Wave could be extremely expensive. Through 2023, when Nvidia’s revenues were expected to at least double, Nvidia’s highest revenue multiple was just 23x forward revenues. So D-Wave definitely looks way overvalued vs. Nvidia.

Note: I used Nvidia as a comparison because D-Wave’s CEO talked about the comparison of possibly using D-Wave’s quantum chip as an alternative to Nvidia’s GPUs.

But on an apples-to-apples basis, D-Wave’s sales multiple of 175x looks a lot more reasonable vs. its pure-play quantum peers.

However, D-Wave does trade at a higher book value multiple of 7.6x relative to itself, and I would have preferred if the book value multiple were trading lower than mid-single digits, especially since management has advised investors that most of that cash is going to be used this year.

With cash set to decrease due to deployment this year, D-Wave’s value of assets on its balance sheet will be pressured, eventually stretching the book value multiple, which is not something I would recommend a buy on just yet.

Then, there is also the added risk that if business does not materialize down the road like management is planning for today, they will have to again go to the markets and dilute current shareholders. D-Wave has been diluting its current shareholder base by raising more capital via secondary share sales, as can be seen in the table below.

Tickers

YTD Dilution

CY24 Dilution

3Yr CAGR Dilution

QBTS

57.2%

39.2%

269.29%

(QUBT)

18.0%

41.0%

49.21%

(RGTI)

61.3%

40.0%

98.95%

IONQ

17.8%

5.2%

15.24%

So, while D-Wave appears to be doing the right things, there definitely are some risks that I am not entirely comfortable with taking on.

Takeaway

D-Wave Computing looks like one of the best picks of the pure-play quantum computing companies available on public markets today. They have a relatively stronger appeal in terms of the commercial viability of their quantum computing systems and services versus their peers and are demonstrating strong growth in bookings. The robust volumes of cash on their balance sheet make this stock difficult to ignore.

But the risks associated so far may put pressure on the valuation of D-Wave, leading me to advocate for a Hold rating on the company.

data.title: Cracker Barrel's Logo Change Misses The Bigger Picture
data.description:
In the last five days alone, Cracker Barrel Old Country Store stock (NASDAQ:CBRL) has fallen by almost 12%. The main reason for this is the rebranding, which was not well received on social media.

Despite the discussions, this seems to me to have become a little too “biased”. Over the past five years, CBRL stock has accumulated a performance of -54%, which is a reflection of the fundamentals. A business overhaul seems like the right move to me. Looking at the financials, it is clear that something needed to be done to try to turn the company around, as was done with Brinker International's Chili's (EAT).

But even though the reaction seems exaggerated to me and I believe that something needs to be done, I don't think we can give them the benefit of the doubt, at least yet.

Cracker Barrel Needed Change

Personally, I found Cracker Barrel's previous logo more charming, but the truth is that this is practically irrelevant to my analysis. The first question to ask is: if so many people liked the previous model, why was the business in decline? In my view, a logo change alone cannot save a business or bury it.

But what is a little clearer to me is that the company should not have stayed the way it was (and here I am not referring to the logo or the political context, but to the business).

Cracker Barrel receives an F in growth and a C- in profitability according to quant ratings. And that's no surprise. The long-term charts below clearly reveal this. For some time now, revenue has been growing at a very slow pace, margins are under a lot of pressure, and free cash flow is very low. This reflects a company that is no longer doing so well operationally, struggling to remain relevant.

What is the solution? Well, it goes far beyond the logo. Taking Chili's as an example, much has been done to improve financials, ranging from marketing to a better value proposition, as well as technology and culinary innovations.

In its presentation, Cracker Barrel explained its plans for restructuring. The initiatives involve refining the brand, but also include more important points such as the menu, value proposition, store remodeling, becoming more relevant in the digital realm, and providing a better experience for employees too.

It is still too early to say whether the implementation of these initiatives will be successful, but some positive points can already be seen. In Q2, the company reported comparable store restaurant sales of 4.7%, which the company attributed to these reforms, as mentioned in the earnings call:

“As these initiatives exemplify how we're evolving the brand by leaning into what makes Cracker Barrel great and doing so in a refined way that appeals to both existing and new guests alike. We're excited about our progress and our teams are energized. Looking back at Q3, the quarter started soft. So we took actions to support the top line and tightly manage our expenses without limiting our ability to deliver our important fourth quarter initiative. [...]

From a culinary perspective, our spring promotion featured 2 shrimp dishes, a bold Louisiana-style shrimp skillet and a comforting Shrimp n' Grits skillet. We also expanded our pancake platform by introducing innovative new flavors and options across various price points as part of our broader barbell strategy. From an operational perspective, we remain focused on strong execution and the metrics that matter [...]”

Considering the financials, all these positive operational metrics and the hints from management in the earnings call already suggest to me that the business is improving.

Will the Logo Change Affect the Business?

With that in mind, I think the market's reaction to the logo change was misguided. In my view, the negative repercussions alone are not enough to be sure that this will impact the company, and at the same time, it makes it seem that a change in the business is not welcome.

Not every change is welcome, and it still depends on the management's execution, but if the initiatives result in better KPIs, this will increase the financials and consequently bring more shareholder value. Regarding the negative repercussions, I believe that only in the coming quarters we will know if this really materialized in lower traffic to the stores or if it was something that stayed more on the internet.

Is It Worth Giving Cracker Barrel The Benefit Of The Doubt?

Although the changes have already shown some positive signs, it is undeniable that Cracker Barrel faces numerous uncertainties. It may be that it will indeed manage to retain more customers and eventually find a more profitable model, just as the reaction on social media may already be a sign that strong growth may not come.

So I don't think it's worth giving Cracker Barrel the benefit of the doubt just yet. It seems much more like a “show me” story to me. And beyond the uncertainties, one of the issues is valuation.

After the decline, the multiples went to much more reasonable levels, such as a forward P/E of 17x, but for a company in turnaround, that seems rich to me.

One way to see if the company is really cheap is to look at its history. The maximum net income margin ever reached was just over 9%, but the average for the last 10 years is much closer to 5%.

Taking the current revenue of $3.5 billion and the margin of 5%, we have a net income of $175 million. If the company manages to return to this, it will be trading at around 7x earnings. In other words, if management manages to do this in 1 or 2 years, and the fair multiple is 14x, this means an implied upside of 100%.

If the company eventually manages to return to this margin and maintain healthy top-line growth in the high single digits, then yes, there is substantial upside to be unlocked. And remember, companies are living entities; they change all the time. Just as they changed their logo, they can revert to the old one, undo the changes, and so on.

But even with this apparent asymmetry, uncertainties detract from the compelling side of the case. I prefer to give a neutral rating and see how the company will fare in this chaotic scenario in the coming quarters, even if it means losing a little profitability if everything goes well.

Therefore, my rating is a hold.

data.title: The Macro Case Proving Why AMLP's 8% Yield Is Built To Last
data.description:
Why I Am Watching AMLP

The Alerian MLP ETF (NYSEARCA:AMLP) has long been on my radar, even after it exited my portfolio because I pivoted from midstream companies to another way of being exposed to key infrastructure, taking a big stake in Prysmian (OTCPK:PRYMY), the global leader in high-voltage cable manufacturing. This doesn't mean I wasn't bullish on midstream, but that I simply saw that an enticing opportunity was arising that I didn't want to miss. AMLP has always stayed on my radar, and I am closely monitoring it because it is likely to find its way back in my portfolio soon. Let me show you why, expanding on the three main considerations I shared a few months ago.

First of all, AMLP is an ETF fully exposed to midstream energy infrastructure. In other words, the companies it holds behave as true gatekeepers of the energy flows through the country. This is why I compared them to the cardiovascular system and explained how irreplaceable their assets are, creating stable, fee-based cash flows that don't only depend on volumes that truly flow through the pipeline but also on volume bookings by energy companies that need to reserve their right to use the pipelines well ahead of when they will know exactly how much gas or oil they will need to transport through them. Most midstream energy companies are MLPs and have, therefore, high distributions. AMLP currently yields 8%, which makes it much better than bonds, even though it has a higher risk. As we can see, the top 10 holdings are among income-seeking investors' favorites, with companies such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Western Midstream Partners (WES), Plains All American Pipeline (PAA), MPLX, and Energy Transfer (ET) all weighing above 12%.

Secondly, MLPs generate K-1 forms, which are cumbersome to manage, especially when held in Roth IRAs. Moreover, foreign investors will face an even more troublesome tax headache when holding a unit of an MLP, with a 37% withholding rate and needing to file a U.S. tax return (Form 1040-NR), in the case they can show they don't owe that tax. AMLP is structured as a C-corp ETF; thus, it handles internally K-1 reporting while paying distributions to shareholders in the form of qualified dividends. This is why its expense ratio is 0.85%, which is high but makes sense when we understand how it simplifies our exposure to MLPs.

Third, AMLP is a large fund, with around $10.5B in AUM. The alternatives are MLPA and MLPX, which have lower fees, more diversification, but smaller AUM. While MLPA yields 7%, MLPX's yield is lower at around 4.7%. The main risk in holding AMLP is seeing a yield cut during an oil crisis or energy downturn (2015 was an example). However, we are seeing a world that is devouring energy, and the rise of AI simply strengthens this trend. We don't have enough renewables, and nuclear power plants take a long time to be built. Natural gas is the go-to solution.

Macro Outlook for Midstream

Just a few days ago, we read that US exports of LNG have reached new highs, with a 22% YoY increase to 12.4 million tons, positioning the U.S. as the largest LNG supplier in the world. Guess where most of this gas is going? Europe, which accounts for two-thirds of total exports. Considering that part of the US-EU trade deal aims at increasing even more LNG exports, I personally see a stable route between the two allied blocks that will secure long-term growth for LNG exports. Now, LNG needs to reach ports. It does so through midstream companies.

But the tale is not only about exports. We see that, since 2019, combined gas use has climbed faster than production. Gas demand is growing, and this is why gas prices are steadily moving up. In particular, we see strong growth in electric power and industrial use, further proof of a reindustrialization process that is spreading across the US.

What does this imply for midstream companies? Well, as the U.S. Energy Information Administration recently explained, "production from the Appalachian Basin in the mid-Atlantic and Ohio region will increasingly meet growing demand on the Gulf Coast in the South Central region, [with] natural gas transitioning through the Eastern Midwest region on the way to the Gulf Coast". The outlook is that LNG exports will grow, generating increasing gas volumes through the midstream infrastructure that transports gas and oil to the Gulf Coast. By the way, even though we hear a lot of talk about the Permian Basin, where gas is a byproduct of crude oil drilling activities, the Appalachian Basin contains the most abundant and cheap-to-access natural gas resources in the U.S. Reserves are estimated to be around 147.3 trillion cubic feet (79.1 is the estimate for the Permian Basin), and production is around 10.5 trillion cubic feet. This means that we are quite safe for almost 15 years.

We have to keep in mind this whole picture to understand why the forecast predicts that, through 2050, the natural gas flows from the Mid-Atlantic and Ohio region to the Eastern Midwest and the South Central region will meaningfully increase. Eastern Midwest will see huge gains in flow volume simply because more gas will be transitioning through this region on its way to the Gulf.

Last but not least, there is a cherry on top of the pie. In the U.S., there are close to 200 natural gas market hubs. They mostly price relative to the Louisiana-based Henry Hub, where the national benchmark price comes from. However, gas prices can vary at the key pricing hubs around the country. We see that the average price was higher in the East, and lower in the South, where it was even negative in the Waha hub. This is because overproduction didn't have enough infrastructure to manage increasing volumes. As a result, major infrastructure expansion projects are being carried out in the region, both to meet current supply and increasing demand.

We know that in 2024, key projects were completed to increase takeaway capacity by 6.5 Bcf/d. Moreover, last year, interstate natural gas capacity increased by over 10 billion cubic feet per day (Bcf/d). This was almost three times the 2023 increase and second only to the 15 Bcf/d we saw in 2021.

Well, the projection of gas prices through 2050 sees something interesting happening. The spot price in the Henry Hub will bifurcate from the Appalachian region supply price. As a result, we can expect that there will be incentives for selling natural gas in Louisiana, rather than in Eastern hubs. This is positive news for midstream companies: they will have to move more gas from the Appalachian basin to the Gulf Coast.

Conclusion

In this article, I wanted to provide further evidence to the bullish stance I took in May. The more I come to realize what huge geopolitical and economic shifts we are going through, the more I think of the surging demand for energy and the key role midstream companies will play for many years to come. As a result, I reiterate my buy rating and inform that I find AMLP as one of the most convincing candidates for a new entry in my portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lockheed Martin: Hard To Change When You're Almost 100 Years Old
data.description:
Next year, Lockheed Corporation will turn 100. Martin Marietta, the other counterparty to the famous 1995 merger, will be 64.

Together, they form Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT), one of the largest defense contractors in the world, maker of the F-35 fighter jet, and many more iconic weapons and defense systems that support the US and its allies.

I recently wrote an article emphasizing the major changes the defense industry is going through, focusing on the rise of defense start ups like Anduril, Palantir (PLTR), or Next Vision (OTCPK:NXSNF).

I believe the recently announced quarter, which included surprising program losses, perfectly depicts Lockheed's problems, as a 100-years-old slow-moving contractor, and why it's hard to see a path for market-beating returns going forward.

Let's dive in.

Introduction

I've been covering Lockheed Martin on Seeking Alpha for a few years now.

Initially, I rated the stock a 'Strong Buy', based on my view that demand for its products and services is set to skyrocket amid geopolitical tensions.

I then downgraded the stock as I've realized that demand isn't the issue here. Rather, Lockheed's limitations as an investment are its margin ceiling, being a cost plus contractor.

Later on, I became even more bearish, as I came to the conclusion that demand might also be weakening, amid a 'Start-upization Of Defense' trend and the rise of new agile players.

Where we currently stand, the stock has moved virtually nowhere during my coverage period, which makes me view my 'Hold' rating as a success.

Now, before we look forward, I think we should go through the bear thesis quickly, and the importance of evolving as investors.

The Bear Thesis & Evolving As Investors

I believe that most investors go through a somewhat similar process. To start, they only invest in indexes. Then, they turn to either dividend investing or momentum investing. Lastly, they either become concentrated stock pickers seeking to beat the market, stay at stage 2, or do some weird combination of everything.

Of course, the type of investing each person chooses to follow can determine whether or not a stock is an attractive addition to their portfolios.

For instance, some investors can make fortunes with names like Palantir, which trade at valuation levels out of this world. Others, would argue that the right way to go is an extremely diversified portfolio of dividend-paying names like Lockheed Martin. And, some people, myself included, would say neither is the way to go.

I consider myself a stock picker with a concentrated portfolio, which includes less than ten stocks. The goal of this portfolio is to beat the market returns consistently, with minimal drawdown risk, and extreme conviction in each stock.

With that framework in mind, I think it makes sense that I'm bearish on Lockheed Martin. Simply put, Lockheed Martin doesn't most of the characteristics I look for in a company.

Lockheed's trajectory is limited to mid-single-digit revenue growth and flat margins due to lack of operational leverage or pricing power. Such trajectory is very unlikely to result in market-beating returns on a consistent basis for a long period.

That is, the bear thesis, in a nutshell.

Second-Quarter Results Only Strengthened The Bear Thesis

Sales in the quarter were $18.2 billion, flat Y/Y, coming below estimates for $18.5 billion. Growth was led my Missiles and Fire Control, which rose 11%, then Space, up 4%, and Aeronautics, up 2%. Rotary and Mission Systems was down 12% Y/Y.

Operating margins dropped sharply, from 11.9% in Q2-24 to 4.1% this quarter, primarily due to recognized program losses in Aeronautics and Rotary and Mission Systems. In Missiles & Fire Control, margins drop 50 bps too, while Space was flat.

Clearly, results in the quarter were very much within the range of the bear thesis framework. In fact, they were even worse.

Looking ahead, Lockheed maintained its sales outlook for the year, but had to make big cuts to its profit outlook due to the $1.8 billion in program charges.

Should they deliver on these targets, it will mark a slowdown from the 5% growth in 2024, but perhaps investors can hang their hats on the fact that this will be a third consecutive year of positive growth. That said, adjusting for inflation, real growth is very muted.

Outlook

After the surprising downfall in Q2, investors are hoping it was a one-time issue, and consensus sees Lockheed returning to its old ways in terms of EPS growth. Meaning that 2026 is set to be a rebound year, which will be followed by a return to ~7% EPS growth.

Based on consensus estimates, EPS growth will be driven by ~4% revenue growth, buybacks, and little to no margin improvements.

I hope that after reading the bear thesis section, this trajectory paints a clear picture of future underperformance. I can't think of any upside surprises to these estimates, given Lockheed's known backlog, pace of deliveries, and stagnating margins.

Right here is where Lockheed falls as an investment prospect for my portfolio. However, it might still fit for two types of investors. Perhaps you're looking to make a short-term gain if valuation is too low, or you're looking to at Lockheed to your dividend portfolio.

Either way, the success of such move is dependant on valuation.

Valuation

At $450 a share, Lockheed trades at 20 times forward earnings, and 15 times next year's EPS estimates. Because this year's EPS estimates are weighed down by the one-off charges, we should focus on 2026 levels.

As we can see, the 15x multiple is roughly in line with the lowest levels Lockheed traded at in recent years. Also, it's about 6% lower than its five-year average valuation.

So, if there was ever a time where a short-term gain could be made with the stock, I believe it's here. And, if you're looking to initiate a long-term dividend position here, I wouldn't be against that either, given the historically low starting valuation.

That said, I still don't see a path for long-term success here.

Conclusion

Just like you wouldn't expect a 100 year old to change their ways, I think investors shouldn't expect a major change in Lockheed Martin's trajectory too.

This is a long-time defense contractor which is going to be here for a long time, but it will grow at a slow pace, with little margin expansion, if any.

These are not the making of a long-term market-beating investment, but investor who are looking for steady dividends or a short-term rebound, Lockheed might fit.

To me, Lockheed remains a 'Hold'.

data.title: The Residential Housing Cycle Has Turned
data.description:
The residential construction cycle is the most important part of the US business cycle, next to manufacturing. Residential construction is highly cyclical, sensitive to changes in monetary policy, and often the first major sector to start a boom or bust.

Many investors and market participants fail to understand that residential housing moves in sequence, like the overall business cycle, and that residential construction does not directly mean home prices.

A sequence evolves through the housing sector that starts with changes in monetary policy, moves through sales volumes and permits, then into actual construction activity and employment, and finally into home prices.

Home prices lag, like inflation, so a downturn in residential construction does not immediately cause a drop in home prices.

In this post, we will go through this sequence, starting with changes in monetary policy, moving through to building permits, analyzing construction activity, breaking down the movements in construction employment, and lastly discussing some national and regional home price trends.

Changes in Monetary Policy

A tightening of monetary policy ignites a downtrend in the residential cycle. This chart shows “real true money supply”, a measure of inflation-adjusted money supply we use at EPB Research.

Peak monetary easing was in February 2021, and major tightening began in mid to late 2022. Monetary policy has been tight, as evidenced by contracting real money supply, but the stock or excess money relative to the pre-COVID trend is likely what has made this downcycle drag out longer than usual.

As the Fed has kept policy tight, excess money, even relative to the muted pre-COVID trend, has been virtually eliminated, which should accelerate the downward momentum that’s already in place.

Permitting Activity

After monetary policy tightens, the first thing to move is sales volumes as well as applications for new building permits.

The peak in easy monetary policy was in February 2021, and building permits peaked three quarters later in December 2021.

Due to the excess money referenced in the above chart, it’s been a slower grind down, but building permits have not stopped with the downward momentum.

As monetary policy remains tight, as evidenced by the still contracting money supply, sales volume will remain weak, and applications for new permits will continue to drop.

Actual Construction Activity

Building permits can change on a dime since it’s simply an application. The number of housing units under construction takes time to turn since construction activity continues until it’s completed.

Peak monetary easing was in February 2021, building permits peaked in December 2021, and housing units under construction peaked in October 2022.

This is the clear residential housing sequence in motion.

While the level of activity remains somewhat elevated with just under 1.4 million units under construction, the downward momentum is precipitous.

Since monetary policy is still tight, sales volume is anemic, and building permits are still dropping, the number of housing units under construction will continue to slide.

From Activity to Employment

Residential construction employment is split between two major categories, building employment and contractor employment.

Building employment is new building plus general contractors for remodeling, while contractors are the sub-contractor specific industries like plumbers and electricians.

Neither category has dropped sharply, but the sub-contractor employment level peaked in late 2024, while building employment has been more firm.

Why Has Employment Remained Stable?

In addition to a strong remodeling cycle, the second and equally important reason as to why employment has held up better than production in the residential sector is profit margins.

Profit margins for the eight largest homebuilders exploded to the highest level in recorded history after the pandemic, hitting 20% in 2022.

As production or activity levels fell but employment did not, profit margins compressed 600bps down to 14%. Any other time in history, a 600bps compression in margins would have led to layoffs.

However, margins were so high that 600bps compression still left companies with a more profitable position than pre-COVID.

Margins are accelerating downwards, and based on the sequence referenced, it will continue. The extremely important wildcard is tariffs.

The tariff impact has not been extreme in the first half of 2025, but if tariff pressure increases in the second half and companies cannot pass the cost through, margin erosion could potentially intensify further, bringing margins below pre-COVID levels and accelerating the job losses that are just begging to appear in the residential construction sector.

Home Prices: Last In The Sequence

At the end of the residential cycle comes home prices – the last to move, just like inflation in the broader business cycle.

Changes in monetary policy lead to a pullback in sales volume and new permits, followed by a downturn in construction activity, a decrease in profit margins, and then a fall in employment. After employment, home prices suffer, which is what we are now starting to see mildly.

On a 3M annualized basis, home prices are dropping 1.5% but as we know, changes in home prices are extremely regional, and national averages don’t capture the full story.

This cycle has come with the most extreme regional differences in history.

Home Price Trends Are Regional

This chart shows the change in active housing inventory by state for 2025 compared to the average of 2018/2019. Orange colors mean higher inventory levels and prices are susceptible to fall, while green colors mean very low inventory and likely still price increases.

Florida and Texas used to have the highest change in inventory, but Colorado has shot to the top of the list.

Biggest Increase in Inventory (2025 vs. 2018/2019):

•Colorado: +37%

•Texas: +34%

•Washington: +30%

•Florida: +30%

•Utah: +27%

Biggest Decrease in Inventory (2025 vs. 2018/2019):

•Connecticut: -72%

•Illinois: -63%

•New Jersey: -61%

•Vermont: -56%

•Rhode Island: -56%

Disparities extend beyond the state-level data and into the metro-level data.

To show one example, the state of Florida has very high inventory in 2025 compared to pre-COVID, with homes moving much more slowly.

Across the entire state, inventory is 30% higher than in 2019 – but that’s not the case in all metro regions.

Key West, Miami-Fort Lauderdale-West Palm Beach, and Naples still have the same or lower inventory compared to pre-COVID.

The inventory explosion in Florida is mainly on the west coast in places like Punta Gorda, Panama City, and Fort Myers.

One more example is California. On average across the state, inventory is 4% lower than pre-pandemic, but differences across metro regions are large.

We can also look at the relative change in home prices for major cities compared to the national average.

In other words, when the line is moving up, it means that city has home prices that are outperforming the national average. When the line is moving lower, that city has home prices underperforming the national average.

This chart shows the three worst-performing cities from the Case-Shiller 20-city data relative to the national average – the cities dragging down the national average.

Dallas, Denver, and San Francisco have home prices that are performing considerably worse in recent months and quarters than the national average.

On the flip side, Charlotte, Miami, and New York are outperforming.

Charlotte has been a strong outperformer since the early days of COVID.

Miami exploded from 2021 through 2024 in absolute and relative terms, but has just started to mildly underperform the national average.

New York was a laggard, suffering during the pandemic, but now outperforming due to extreme inventory issues.

The Residential Housing Cycle Has Turned

Overall, the residential housing cycle moves in a sequence, and that sequence remains in a solid downturn - although it takes time for the full sequence to evolve and this cycle has been more extreme in that regard.

With employment starting to soften, we see signs of the last phase in the sequence – home price declines – but the declines are extremely different across states.

States with a lot of building have high inventory, and homes are sitting longer, but many parts of the country have inventory issues, which is keeping prices elevated.

The most problematic states include Florida, Texas, Colorado, Washington, Arizona, Utah, and Nevada.

Tight inventory and upward price pressure still exist across the Northeast and Midwest.

The key takeaway from this report is that the residential housing cycle follows a sequence.

It’s critical to analyze the entire cycle as a process rather than select one part of the sequence, like home prices, to make a claim about the residential cycle in general.

data.title: FFLC: Measured Exposure For Growth With Valuation Discipline
data.description:
With a bottom-up investment style, the Fidelity Fundamental Large Cap Core ETF (BATS:FFLC) has structured a portfolio that sticks to well-known big tech market leaders. However, the fund maintains a targeted sector and stock selection that still allows it to trade at a small discount to broad equity market indexes. In fact, the fund has a slightly higher growth-oriented profile compared to some peers, which is likely to continue to do well going forward, absent any major shifts in market sentiment.

ETF Description & Highlights

FFLC provides exposure to companies that constitute the S&P 500 and Russell 1000 indexes with strong fundamentals. The fund adopts a hybrid style, incorporating growth and value themes in its research methodology. The stock selection is based on a bottom-up approach that assesses companies' financial health, earnings outlook, competitive positioning in their respective industries, and overall market conditions.

As of August 19, 2025, FFLC had $816.2 million in assets invested in a high-conviction portfolio of 108 holdings, with an average market cap of $347.8 billion. The fund has a strong bias toward mega caps, which make up 48.5% of total assets, surpassing the 42.8% allocation in the Russell 1000 index, here represented by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB). Large caps come next, accounting for 29.5%, while mid caps constitute 20.3%.

FFLC’s top holdings are concentrated in mega caps from the technology sector, with NVIDIA (8.2%), Microsoft (6.9%), Apple (3.5%), and Broadcom (2.8%) together accounting for more than 21% of total assets. Meta Platforms (5.6%), Amazon (4.4%), and Alphabet (3.8%) are also mega caps with a significant weighting in the fund, mirroring to some extent traditional market cap indexes. Bank of America (2.3%) and Wells Fargo (2.3%) represent financials, while Exxon Mobil (2.2%) completes the top ten holdings, adding exposure to the energy sector.

The next table presents a list of six ETFs that incorporate fundamental analysis in their investment decision process, and will serve as the peer group for FFLC in this article. The first is the Schwab Fundamental U.S. Large Company ETF (FNDX), with around $19 billion in assets, which selects and weights stocks based on measures like sales, cash flow, and dividends. The VanEck Morningstar Wide Moat ETF (MOAT) focuses on firms with a so-called wide moat competitive advantage, according to the definition of Morningstar’s research team, resulting in an unusual mid-cap tilt compared to other peers. The Invesco RAFI US 1000 ETF (PRF), one of the larger funds in this group at about $8 billion, tracks a fundamentally weighted index of the largest 1,000 U.S. companies, according to measures like book value, cash flow, sales, and dividends. Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Core ETF (FELC) takes an active-management approach, using factors to adjust exposures, with the S&P 500 as its benchmark. Finally, the Distillate U.S. Fundamental Stability & Value ETF (DSTL) applies a more selective approach, screening for high free-cash-flow stability and relatively low leverage, resulting in a concentrated portfolio with substantial allocation to mid-cap companies, while the iShares Morningstar U.S. Equity ETF (ILCB) is a fairly small fund with $1.1 billion in assets that tracks the Morningstar U.S. Large-Mid Cap Index.

FFLC has a large exposure to technology (30.3%), which stands well above all other sectors. The fund’s second-largest sector is financial services (15.4%), followed by communication services (11.7%) and industrials (11.2%). Sectors like consumer discretionary (9.6%) and healthcare (8.0%) are still representative for the fund, while it has smaller positions in energy (4.4%), consumer staples (4.0%), utilities (2.3%), basic materials (1.7%), and real estate (1.5%). FFLC’s allocation is relatively aligned with market-cap equity indexes. The fund is only slightly overweight in industrials (+2.7%), financial services (+1.9%), and communication services (+1.7%), with similar underweight positions in technology (-3.3%), consumer discretionary (-1.4%), and consumer staples (-1.1%).

The allocation to technology is spread across a dozen holdings. The sector tilts heavily toward semiconductors through shares of NVIDIA, Broadcom, and Marvell Technology. Software also plays a central role, anchored by Microsoft and Salesforce, while Apple remains one of the fund’s core holdings. In addition, the sector exposure is complemented by Western Digital in data storage and the communication network through Cisco Systems.

In financial services, the fund shows wider diversification compared with technology. The sector’s core holdings are diversified banks like Wells Fargo and Bank of America. Payment processors Visa and Mastercard are also relatively large positions, as well as property insurers Travelers and Chubb. On the other hand, the fund still has lighter exposure to regional banks like M&T Bank and United Community Banks.

Communication services is another sector where FFLC has a significant allocation, but concentrated in only four stocks. Behemoths like Meta Platforms and Alphabet are the largest positions, complemented by cable and broadband provider Comcast and streaming player Netflix.

Outside of the top three sectors, FFLC adds diversification through allocations to a range of industries. Amazon is the key position in consumer discretionary, while Exxon Mobil and Cheniere Energy represent the energy sector, and UnitedHealth Group, Cigna, and Boston Scientific offer exposure to the healthcare sector. Meanwhile, the fund takes a broad-based approach in industrials with shares across aerospace, machinery, and engineering segments through names like GE Aerospace, Boeing, Westinghouse Air Brake, and Quanta Services.

FFLC has a growth bias compared to peers. The fund overweights technology (+4.7%), while the peer group has a greater exposure to healthcare and consumer staples, leading to |FFLC’s lighter allocation of -6.1% and -3.6% in these two sectors, respectively. This reflects PRF (16.3%), FNDX (17.6%), and DSTL (21.8%) conservative stance in technology. On the flip side, healthcare is a core position in funds like DSTL (23.0%) and MOAT (20.7%).

Still, FFLC has a high sector correlation with ILCB (0.98) and FELC (0.98), while correlation with MOAT (0.72) and DSTL (0.62) is moderate. In these two funds, FFLC’s individual stock overlap is weak at below 11%, underscoring quite specific stock picking methodologies, despite sharing a common fundamentally driven orientation.

Valuation Slightly Below The Benchmark

Trading at a 21.9x P/E, FFLC is 5.8% cheaper than the Russell 1000’s 23.2x, reflecting sector allocation and stock selection. Sure, the fund’s underweight exposure to technology (-3.3%) and consumer discretionary (-1.4%) plays a role here. On top of that, its P/E profile in these two sectors and in consumer staples is over 15% lower than the Russell 1000, as the fund holds no shares in high-multiple companies in these sectors, such as Tesla, Palantir, Costco, and Walmart.

FFLC shows stronger growth measures compared to the broad market. Its historical earnings growth (9.7%) outpaces the Russell 1000 (7.3%), as well as its sales growth of 8.5% versus 7.5% for the benchmark. This is driven by overweight positions in NVIDIA, Amazon, and Microsoft, which have delivered at least 30% EPS growth over the past few years.

Meanwhile, on profitability, while FFLC’s EBITDA margin is similar to the Russell 1000, it exhibits weaker average operational efficiency, as measured by its ROE of 35.1%, a 9.7 percentage points gap relative to the benchmark. This reflects relatively lower ROE across several sectors, including healthcare, industrials, consumer discretionary, and staples, which are a drag on the fund’s average and only partially offset by large positions in high-margin big tech names.

FFLC has the second-highest valuation in the peer group, only below ILCB’s 23.7x P/E, as ILCB has a concentrated exposure to high-multiple names in the technology sector. On the flip side, funds like FNDX (16.3x P/E), PRF (16.4x), and DSTL (15.0x) have a stronger bias toward value, due to their lower exposure to technology and communication services. Meanwhile, their stock selection in healthcare is a positive contributor to these funds’ low valuations as well. In summary, considering ILCB as an outlier within the group, FFLC is positioned at the higher end of the valuation spectrum. This makes sense if we look at the fund’s average earnings growth compared to FNDX, MOAT, and PRF’s slower growth profile.

Outperforming The Benchmark and Peers

After a big win in 2022, when FFLC remained flat while the broad market declined nearly 20%, the fund has continued to do well in general, staying ahead of the Russell 1000 since 2024. To be sure, that strong 2022 is the biggest contributor to FFLC’s outperformance in both the 3- and 5-year time frames. However, FFLC’s strong returns in 2024 and year-to-date underscore its positioning toward growth.

FFLC has been the top-performing fund within peers over the past three and five years, not only because of 2022, but also due to 2024 and year-to-date returns, followed by FELC and ILCB, also growth-oriented funds.

With a beta of 1.01 (3-year) and 0.93 (5-year), FFLC’s volatility has been slightly lower than the Russell 1000. This has translated into more contained price action than the broad market, particularly on the downside, given its capture ratio below 100.

The peer group has seen an uneven picture with regard to volatility, though. FNDX, PRF, and DSTL have a more defensive profile, with betas under 0.95, while MOAT and ILCB have been more volatile than the broad market. This leaves FFLC in the middle ground of the peer group, as the peers’ fundamentals-oriented approaches have led to distinct strategic positioning, as discussed earlier in the portfolio section.

Trading about $3.9 million a day, FFLC falls behind heavy volume funds like FNDX, MOAT, and FELC, while its bid-ask average spread of 0.09% is not as narrow as the more liquid peers. Meanwhile, FFLC’s turnover of 63% is at the high end of the group, leading to an expense ratio of 0.38%, which is somewhat expected for an actively managed fund. The fund also offers a relatively low TTM yield (0.91%), driven by its underweight exposure to sectors like healthcare, consumer staples, and real estate. FFLC’s one and three-year tracking errors are good under 4%, below the peer's average. Finally, its Sharpe ratio of 1.02 (3-year) and 1.08 (5-year) is by far the best in the group, as the other top-performing funds, FELC and ILCD, sit below the 0.85 level. These off-chart readings are largely driven by FFLC performance in 2022. However, I would say that even without it, the fund would still outperform, given its stronger returns in the past two years, combined with an overall modest volatility.

Final Thoughts

FFLC’s investment approach has resulted in a portfolio with a growth orientation, leaning on big techs, but with a sector exposure and stock selection that make the fund trade at a small discount to the Russell 1000. It also gives the fund an above-average growth orientation compared to some peers with more conservative styles, such as FNDX, PRF, and DSTL.

Looking ahead, the fund seems well aligned with the current cautiously optimistic mindset, where earnings growth continues to be a catalyst for the equity market amid well-known headwinds, such as inflationary pressures and tariff headlines. While FFLC may be more vulnerable to shifts in market sentiment, especially if earnings growth disappoints at some point, my view is that the balance of risks is favorable for the fund’s positioning, which warrants an overweight allocation for growth-oriented investors.

data.title: Eli Lilly Vs. Novo Nordisk: One Stock Is Massively Undervalued, The Other Overvalued
data.description:
The GLP-1 and weight loss medication era has put Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) into the spotlight in recent months. Both stocks declined significantly and at a first glance, they both look undervalued compared to their historical averages after the introduction of weight loss medication.

I however think that currently only one of the two is offering substantial upside, when actually looking at the numbers. Let's jump right into it.

Current Situation

When looking at the different business segments of the two companies and how the revenue is distributed between them, both generate approx. 20% of their revenue with Obesity medication.

Eli Lilly is currently generating revenues of ~$5.7 billion while Novo is currently at ~$3.9 billion in this segment. Lilly is also more diversified with their Immunology and Oncology segment while Novo is more focused on the Diabetes market.

Segment Revenue [mill. USD] Share
Diabetes Care 14,857 52.5%
Obesity care (Zepbound) 5,693 20.1%
Immunology 2,343 8.3%
Neuroscience 616 2.2%
Oncology 4,361 15.4%
Other 414 1.5%

Eli Lilly Revenue per Segment as of Q2/2025

Segment Revenue [mill. DKK] Share
Diabetes Care 98,994 74.8%
GLP-1 72,017 54.4%
Insulin 26,977 20.4%
Obesity Care 24,939 18.9%
Wegovy 21,036 15.9%
Saxenda 3,903 3.0%
Total Rare Diseases 8,360 6.3%

Novo Nordisk Revenue per Segment as of Q2/2025

Like mentioned in the intro, both companies experienced a continuous sell-off in the last months, with Novo down ~61% from all-time highs and Lilly ~25%.

What Caused The Sell-Offs?

There have been plenty of reasons for the sell-offs:

Efficacy Uncertainty:

Lilly and Novo are currently battling for the best efficacy in the weight loss segment.

Novo's weight loss drug CagriSema didn't meet expectations, as they only achieved an average weight loss in the range of 15.7% to 22.7% over 68 weeks, compared to the aimed for 25%. This is however pretty much in line with the ~21% in 72 weeks of Lilly's Zepound.

On the other hand Lilly's oral weight loss medication Orforglipron is worse than Novo's oral therapy at the moment. (12.4% in 72 weeks vs. 13.6% in 62 weeks)

After each announcement and new clinical trial result one company jumps 10% while the other loses a similar amount. I believe this volatility will continue in the following months.

I however think that they are both very well positioned right now. One percent more efficacy won't influence the consumer decision in my opinion. Way more important are potential side effects and pricing.

Further Competition:

In addition to the fierce competition among each other, other player are also vying for market share in the promising weight loss segment.

Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) for example is currently developing several obesity treatments in their pipeline. Their most promising and advanced weight loss drug (CT-388) is currently achieving an average loss of 18.8% in 24 weeks. It is currently in clinical development phase 2 out of 3.

Hims and Hers (HIMS) is also actively exploring generic products and other options to continue selling weight loss medication after their collaboration with Novo ended, especially in the countries where patents are running out.

Patents:

Novo Nordisk's main markets are protected until 2031. However in other key markets such as Canada and China their patent is losing protection as soon as 2026.

Lilly's patent on the other hand is stretching further - to at least 2039 - in the US. This is expanding their wiggle room but also limits their time horizon to some kind.

Novo on the other hand filed a patent for treating Alzheimer's with their already existing weight loss drug Semaglutide. A large phase 3 clinical trial - ending September 2025 - is expected to deliver first results in this matter. Should this be successful a completely new business opportunity is generated for Novo.

Just for context: There is currently no meaningful treatment for Alzheimer's, only medication that helps with the symptoms. This market alone is already estimated to be as big as $5.5 billion and is expected to grow at rates of 15% in the next years. So even a low efficacy should generate a multibillion dollar opportunity for Novo.

Geopolitical Turbulence:

The mood surrounding NVO and LLY has worsened by President Trump's remarks on the possible annexation of Greenland and especially the planned trade tariffs on goods made outside the US.

Additionally Trump's administration is pushing companies to adjust prices globally which could have negative impacts on the global sales and distribution.

Valuation

This kind of environment definitely justified some kind of sell-off, but 62% in Novo's case and 25% for Lilly seem a bit excessive. Traditional and more static valuation metrics show that Novo might currently be cheaper than Lilly.

But let's look at the actual numbers and especially also factor in the other segments and their respective growth prospects.

First up we will calculate the growth needed for each company to justify their current valuation. For this we need some additional assumptions:

Metric Eli Lilly Novo Nordisk
2025 Revenue Average of 2025 Guidance: $61 billion TTM Revenue: $49 billion
EBIT Margin Average of 2025 Guidance: 43% Average of the last 4 years: 44%
Cash Conversion The best rate of the last 4 years: 2021: 68% Average of the last 4 years:76%
WACC 9%
Perpetuity Growth 3.5%

I'm more conservative with my Novo assumptions to adjust for their lower diversification as well as the shorter patent runway of Wegovy.

Required Growth

Eli Lilly

With these assumptions and the current market cap of $629 billion, Eli Lilly needs to growth with 18% p.a. in the time frame of 2026 to 2032 to justify their current valuation.

Novo Nordisk

Novo on the other hand just needs to achieve growth rates of 1% p.a. after 2025 to justify their current market cap of $241 billion.

But let's analyse the achievable growth for both companies and see if Eli Lilly's premium might be deserved.

Achievable Growth

Eli Lilly

Like mentioned above Eli Lilly is generating revenues in the following business segments. Experts are currently also predicting the following market growth rates for each respective segment:

Segment Revenue [mill. USD] Share Expected Market CAGR
Diabetes Care 14,857 52.5% 12%
Obesity care 5,693 20.1% 25%
Immunology 2,343 8.3% 10%
Neuroscience 616 2.2% 5%
Oncology 4,361 15.4% 16%
Other 414 1.5% 0%

By weighing these growth rates with the current revenue share, we get an expected top-line growth rate of 15.8% for the whole business of Eli Lilly.

By plugging this into our DCF we currently get an overvaluation of 11% for Eli Lilly.

Novo Nordisk

We can now do the same for Novo Nordisk, here I assumed a bit more pessimistic growth rates to reflect the current negative sentiment surrounding the company.

Segment Revenue [mill. DKK] Share Expected Market CAGR
Diabetes Care 98,994 74.8% 10%
Obesity Care 24,939 18.9% 20%
Total Rare Diseases 8,360 6.3% 0%

The rather conservative assumptions still give us an anticipated growth rate of 11.3% for the whole business of Novo Nordisk. Even with these we currently get a fair value market cap of $427 billion, indicating potential upside of up to 77%.

Conclusion

The market is wrong right now. The premium of Lilly compared to Novo Nordisk is too high at the moment and not justified. Even after assuming rather conservative assumptions for Novo and more optimistic ones for Lilly, Novo looks severely undervalued, while Lilly seems to be slightly overvalued.

At current prices Novo Nordisk is a Strong Buy. It's good positioning in the promising weight loss segment, solid foundation in the diabetes market and the future potential of the Alzheimer's treatment paired with a potential undervaluation of up to 77% make the company a compelling pick right now.

Eli Lilly is a high quality company that is very well positioned to benefit from the growing weight loss market. Their other segments - especially Oncology and Immunology - also seem promising. At current prices, I however believe that there are better opportunities in the market, which is why I rate Lilly a Hold. I personally would wait for an additional decline of ~10, which would make Lilly fairly valued right now, to open a position.

data.title: Stryker Keeps Beating Expectations, Yet Investors Should Stay Cautious
data.description:
In December 2024 I wrote about Stryker Corporation (NYSE:SYK) for the first and only time. And similar to many other medical technology companies – like Edwards Lifesciences (EW) – I considered Stryker Corporation to be overvalued. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

“Stryker Corporation is certainly a great business with a wide economic moat around its business. The company is growing at a solid pace and is also performing well. Right now, the major problem for Stryker is the valuation of the stock. In order to be fairly valued right now, the stock has to grow at a high pace and, in my opinion the necessary growth rates are not realistic.

In my opinion, the stock must drop at least $100 lower than the current stock price in order to be interesting as an investment. Until this happens, the stock can only remain on the watchlist and find its place among many other high-quality businesses that are just a little too expensive right now.”

I rated the stock as a “Hold” and since my last article was published about eight months ago, the stock is trading more or less for the same price – the stock increased about 2% in value (while the S&P 500 increased 7% in the same timeframe).

And while the stock is trading for almost the same price as in December 2024, the business might have changed during this time and a stock price that was not justified back then, might be justified now. We start by looking at the last quarterly results.

Great Quarterly Results

On July 31, 2025, Stryker reported second quarter results for fiscal 2025 and did beat analysts’ expectations for revenue as well as earnings per share. Looking at the results year-over-year, net sales increased 11.1% year-over-year from $5,422 million in Q2/24 to $6,022 million in Q2/25. Operating income increased also from $1,051 million in the same quarter last year to $1,113 million in this quarter – resulting in 5.9% year-over-year growth. Diluted earnings per share increased from $2.14 in Q2/24 to $2.29 in Q2/25 – resulting in 7.0% year-over-year growth.

Looking at results in more detail, we can see sales being driven by two major trends. First, when looking at the geographic regions, growth was especially driven by the United States. Not only are the United States responsible for about 75% of total revenue, revenue increased 12.5% year-over-year while International revenue increased only 6.8% year-over-year.

Second, we can look at the two major business segments – MedSurg and Neurotechnology as well as Orthopaedics. The latter segment generated $2,251 million in revenue and year-over-year it was only 2.0% growth. The biggest part of growth stemmed from MedSurg and Neurotechnology, which grew 17.3% year-over-year to a quarterly revenue of $3,771 million in revenue.

When looking at the different sub-segments, especially Vascular reported high growth rates of 52.3%. Neuro Cranial increased 19.8% in revenue year-over-year and Endoscopy increased 17.1% year-over-year.

Without much doubt, Stryker reported great quarterly results, but revenue growth is still similar to the last few quarters and unless growth rates are accelerating, we still must see Stryker overvalued. Fundamental business improved in the last few quarters, but this is still not enough to justify the current share price.

Raising Full-year Guidance

Aside from reporting solid results, Stryker also raised its full year 2025 outlook. Stryker is now expecting organic net sales growth to be between 9.5% and 10% due to a strong demand for the products and the company’s operational momentum. The company is also expecting the adjusted operating margin to expand by about 100 basis points. The company is also expecting adjusted earnings per share to be in the range of $13.40 to $13.60.

However, when considering the question we raised above – Is Stryker a “Buy” now – the answer still has to be the same. With growth rates still being around 10%, the improvement is not enough to justify the current stock price and neither the last quarter results nor the raised guidance for fiscal 2025 are enough to see Stryker as a “Buy” now.

Growth

While quarterly results and guidance for the fiscal year are certainly important pieces of information, we should not make long-term investment decisions based on this information. Instead, we should pay a close attention to the growth expectations for the years to come. To justify the current stock price, Stryker should be able to grow at a higher pace for several years and actually improve its business in a far-reaching way.

A strong hint for growth rates accelerating in the next few years are analysts’ assumptions for revenue as well as earnings per share. When looking at the revision data, we see estimates for the next few years being constantly raised by analysts, which is a strong sign for an improving and healthy business.

Not only are analysts constantly raising estimates for the next few years – they are also expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 11.25% for the years between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2030.

When looking at Stryker growth potential in the years to come, we can mention the $450 million modification from the Defense Logistics Agency. Stryker has been awarded a maximum $450 million extension to an existing contract, which will run until July 2030. We should not belittle this contract, however for a business generating $6 billion in quarterly sales, a $450 million contract over 5 years is just a drop in the bucket and certainly not enough to accelerate growth and keep growth rates high.

During the last earnings call, management talked a lot about new launches and the term “launch” was mentioned 47 times, for example:

The launches of Mako Spine and shoulder are going well and remain on track for full launches as discussed on our last earnings call. Our platform launches such as LIFEPAK 35 and the Pangea plating system continue to have success in the marketplace and are driving meaningful contributions to growth. We recently received approval for LIFEPAK 35 in Europe and are on track to launch in late Q3.

Of course, launching new products will lead to additional revenue from these new products, but not necessarily to higher growth rates for the business as new products often cannibalize revenue of former products and Stryker not only has to continue growing with a high pace but rather has to accelerate growth rates.

Acquisitions

In February 2025, Stryker announced that it has completed the acquisition of Inari Medical Inc., which is providing innovative solutions for venous thromboembolism clot removal without the use of thrombolytic drugs. The business was acquired for almost $5 billion and the company is hoping that the business will be a good addition to the fast-growing VTE segment.

Aside from the major acquisition of Inari, Stryker also made six acquisitions in 2024 and the acquired companies included NICO Corporation, Vertos Medical, Care.ai, Molli Surgical, Artelon and SERF SAS. For most of these acquisitions we don’t know details about the purchasing price, but we are mostly talking about smaller acquisitions. Rumors claim a purchasing price of $300 million for Artelon and SERF was obviously acquired for $245 million (some sources say $246 million, other claim $244 million).

Acquisitions certainly contribute to higher growth rates. However, these are rather small acquisitions and the impact on Stryker’s top line is limited by business that generate revenue in the double-digit millions at best. We can also make the argument that acquisitions might have synergies in the quarters to come, which are not visible right now. By reducing costs and becoming more effective, Stryker might improve its operating margin and therefore earnings per share could grow with a higher pace.

However, I am rather pessimistic about synergy effects as they often don’t materialize. Aside from several well-known examples where acquisitions didn’t have the positive synergy effects investors hoped for, a lot of research is demonstrating that synergy effects occur only for a small part of acquisitions. Boston Consulting Group is writing in an article on the topic:

Academic research has repeatedly confirmed that about two-thirds of all mergers and acquisitions among public companies destroy value for the acquirer, at least in the short term. Even when acquirers justify deals by pointing out the ample synergy opportunities that they offer, capital markets remain skeptical. Yet a significant minority of acquisitions do manage to create value for the owners of both the acquirer and the target, demonstrating that despite the doubters in the capital markets, the overriding M&A rationale—value creation—remains valid.

Hence, I would not bet on Stryker growing at a much higher pace due to acquisition and potential synergy effects that might emerge in the coming quarters.

Additional Risks due to Tariffs

And not only do I not see Stryker being able to accelerate growth rates, the announcement of tariffs and the resulting uncertainty is adding additional risks for the business and growth potential in the years to come. Right now, management is expecting a negative impact of $175 million due to tariffs in fiscal 2025.

It is true that the negative impact of tariffs for fiscal 2025 is already reflected in the updated and raised guidance, but not only will the negative impact continue in the next few years. The high levels of uncertainty and the risk of tariffs fluctuating again (and potentially being higher again) is making it more difficult to growth with a high pace (or even accelerate growth).

Still Overvalued

In a final step, we are calculating an intrinsic value for the stock. In December 2024, when my last article was published, Stryker was trading for 39 times earnings and 44 times free cash flow, which is certainly not cheap, but was in line with the long-term average valuation multiples for the stock.

Right now, Stryker is trading for 50 times earnings and 36 times free cash flow, which is above the average P/E ratio and below the average P/FCF ratio. Nevertheless, both metrics are not really indicating that Stryker is a bargain or fairly valued.

And when trying to determine an intrinsic value by using a discount cash flow calculation, we come to a similar conclusion and Stryker still seems to be overvalued. We are calculating with a discount rate of 10% once again (as this is the annual return we like to achieve at least) and with 386.4 million diluted shares outstanding (the last reported number). As basis for our calculation, we can use the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $4,024 million.

When calculating with these assumptions, Stryker must grow 11% annually for the next ten years followed by 6% growth till perpetuity to be fairly valued right now. Or the company must grow its free cash flow between 15% and 16% for the next ten years followed by 4% growth till perpetuity.

Now we can argue that Stryker grew its earnings per share with a CAGR of 19.20% in the last ten years, but these numbers are misleading. Earnings per share in 2014 were exceptionally low – leading to a high CAGR when calculating for the timeframe between fiscal 2014 and fiscal 2024. More realistic are growth rates in the high single digits or very low double digits for earnings per share. But I would not calculate with more than 10% or 11% growth for the next ten year.

But no matter what growth assumptions we are using, Stryker is at best fairly valued and certainly not a bargain at this point in time.

Conclusion

Stryker is certainly a great business with a proven business model and a wide economic moat around the business. Additionally, we can expect high single digit or even double digit growth rates in the years to come. But the stable business model and high growth rates are not enough for Stryker to be a “Buy” now. The company would actually have to accelerate its growth rates to be undervalued, and I don’t see where this growth might stem from.

We can expect Stryker to perform well and grow with a high pace in the years to come, but this is not enough to call the stock a good “Buy”. Current growth rates only justify another “Hold” rating and I would still argue the stock has to decline about $100 before we should think about an investment.
